## What Exists
### Data Layer

-- **10 tasks** downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video/audio editors + producers; understanding: private detectives).
-- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output to score against yet).

+- 10 tasks downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video editors, audio/video technicians, producers/directors; understanding: private detectives).
+- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output — these get attacks and rollouts but no Axis 1 gap).
- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.
### Viewer (`viewer/`)
- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
-- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into sortable table with score pills and tags.
-- Fetches rollouts manifest from `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` (optional — graceful degradation when absent).
-- Dark theme, sticky headers, responsive layout.
+- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into a sortable table with score pills and tags.
+- Rollouts tab reads `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` and shows each policy's submit status, round count, and produced files.
### Eval Framework (`eval/`)

-**Philosophy**: Give the judge a fair shot. Early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to things the judge simply couldn't see (codec, resolution, runtime, exact wording, audio DSP). Deterministic fact tools close that gap — the remaining score shortfall is genuine rubric defects or unverifiable provenance claims.
+One entrypoint, `eval.run --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}`, built on a shared judge harness, model registry, and null/attack generators.

-**Judge agent loop** (`eval/harness.py`): The judge is a tool-calling agent. It receives the task description, the rubric (per-item ids, point values, criterion text), and a structured file manifest split into two role-labelled groups: `deliverable` (the graded output) and `reference` (read-only inputs the worker was given). Role-scoping in the bundle resolver prevents the judge from accidentally grading reference files as if they were the deliverable. The agent calls tools freely until it exhausts a 30-call budget or 12 rounds, then is forced to call `submit_scorecard`. A top-up pass re-asks for any missing items if fewer than 95% are scored. `judge_safe` wraps every call so a single API failure cannot abort a sweep.

+**Judge harness (`harness.py` + `tools.py`)**: the judge is a tool-calling agent. It gets the task prompt, the rubric, and a role-labelled file manifest (`deliverable` vs read-only `reference`), then calls tools until it submits a per-item verdict (`met` / `unmet` / `partial` / `unverifiable`). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media` via ffprobe, `file_facts`, `analyze_audio`, `compare_audio_sync`, scene-cut frame sampling) exist so the judge never has to guess codec/resolution/runtime/loudness/sync — early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to exactly that gap.

-**Scoring**: Per-item verdicts (met/unmet/partial/unverifiable) → normalized score (positive ceiling only) and `verifiable_only` score (dropping uncheckable items). Penalty items (negative score) are floored at 0; the `penalty_floored` flag signals when the raw score crossed zero.
+**Judge / policy model registry (`models.py`)** now spans 9 models chosen to vary three things at once:
-**Available judge tools** (gated by the model's declared modalities):
+| key | provider | modalities | role |
+| `gpt-mini` / `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | text+image | capability ablation (strong vs weak, same vendor) |
+| `qwen-vl` / `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | text+image / text-only | modality ablation (same family, vision on/off) |
+| `gemini-lite` / `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | text+image+**audio** | native-audio judges (free 2nd one for A/V cross-checks) |
+| `gemma4` / `qwen36` / `mimo` | OpenRouter | text+image(+audio for `mimo`) | rollout **policies** — agents that attempt the real tasks |
-| Tool | Modality gate | Description |
-| `list_files` | always | Flat listing of every deliverable file, including contents of zip archives |
-| `list_reference_files` | always | Listing of reference/input files (comparison only, never graded) |
-| `read_text` | always | Extracts text from PDF / DOCX / TXT / MD / SRT deliverable files |
-| `read_reference` | always | Reads text of a reference file, prefixed `[REFERENCE INPUT]` to prevent grade confusion |
-| `file_facts` | always | Deterministic: exact extension, type, size in KB, PDF page count, word count — use for "is it a PDF?", "≤2 pages?", word limits |

-| `probe_media` | always | ffprobe ground truth: container, codec, exact resolution, fps, duration in seconds, audio sample rate, bit depth (e.g. `pcm_s24le` = 24-bit) — use for codec/resolution/runtime/sample-rate criteria |

-| `analyze_audio` | always | DSP measurement: integrated loudness (LUFS), true peak (dBFS), tempo (BPM), estimated musical key, key confidence, harmonic fraction, spectral centroid — use for tempo/key/loudness/instrumental criteria instead of guessing |

-| `compare_audio_sync` | always | Onset-envelope cross-correlation against a reference track: start offset (ms), tail offset (ms), end-to-end drift (ms) — use for "synchronized to" and "drift ≤N ms" criteria |
-| `transcribe_audio` | always | Whisper speech-to-text transcript of an audio or video file's audio track |
-| `view_pdf_page` | image | Renders a PDF page (0-indexed) to JPEG and attaches it — use for layout / formatting criteria |
-| `view_image` | image | Attaches an image file from the deliverable |
-| `view_reference` | image | Attaches a reference image or PDF page (for comparison only) |

-| `view_video_frames` | image | Scene-cut frames by default (one per detected shot boundary, best for "includes a shot of X" / face checks); pass `start_seconds`/`end_seconds` for uniform windowed sampling (e.g. to read an on-screen text card) |
-| `listen_audio` | audio | Natively listens to an audio clip (base64 MP3, ≤90 s) — for subjective timbre / mix / tone criteria; available only to native-audio models (e.g. Gemini) |
-| `submit_scorecard` | always | Submits per-item verdicts (met / unmet / partial / unverifiable) and short justification notes — called exactly once when done |

+**Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)**: per task, a `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF / silent stem zip) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is, unrevised) — two cheap anchors for the bottom of Axis 1.
-**Judge Suite** (cheap, varied by capability):
-| key | model | modalities |
-| `gpt-mini` | gpt-5.4-mini | text + image |
-| `gpt-nano` | gpt-5.4-nano | text + image |
-| `qwen-vl` | qwen/qwen3.5-35b-a3b | text + image |
-| `qwen-text` | qwen/qwen3-30b-a3b-instruct | text only |
-| `gemini-lite` | gemini-2.5-flash-lite | text + image + audio |
+**Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2, new this pass**: cheap, deterministic, no-LLM-in-the-loop deliverables designed to raise the rubric score without doing the work:
+- `restate_input` (PDF) — echo the prompt + reference text back as the "report."
+- `keyword_stuff` (PDF) — emit every quoted literal and de-verbed clause from the rubric itself, i.e. use the rubric as its own answer key.
+- `presence_max` (video) — black/source video with an oversized burned-in title and a caption bar covering the frame: presence without the matching absence condition.
+- `stems_named` (audio archive) — correctly-named stems that are pure silence: presence-only "includes an X stem" criteria pass for free.

-**Modes**: `--mode judge` (score rubrics on subjects: gold, null blank, null unedited_input, or rollout deliverables) and `--mode rollout` (policy agents attempt real tasks, editing media via sandboxed ffmpeg).

+A positive item any attack passes is a **leak**, surfaced the same way as null-leaks. Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2`: gold scores 0.92–0.96 across judge runs, `keyword_stuff` scores 0.89–0.92, and `restate_input` scores 0.74–0.80 — **44 of 45 positive items leak to mechanical attacks**, i.e. ~90%+ of the score is buyable with zero investigative work.
-**Null inputs**: Auto-generated per task — `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is). Two cheap anchors to calibrate the bottom of the scale.

+**Adversarial agent eval (`adv_eval.py`) — new this pass**: goes one step further than mechanical attacks. An LLM agent is told the rubric and asked to game it (not do the work) while a separate **blind pairwise judge** — shown only anonymous "A"/"B" submissions, position-randomized, never told which is gold — independently rates which is actually better work. Two numbers per attack: `rubric_score` (how well it games the rubric) and `gold_winrate` (how often the blind judge still prefers gold). Their product, `deception`, is high only when an attack both games the rubric **and** is empirically worse:
-### Results (`eval/results/`)
+| task | attack | rubric_score | gold_winrate | deception |
+| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | `presence_max` (mechanical) | 0.22 | 1.00 | 0.22 |
+| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | adversarial agent | 0.93 | 0.50 | 0.466 |
+| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `restate_input` (mechanical) | 0.80 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
+| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `keyword_stuff` (mechanical) | 0.92 | 1.00 | 0.92 |
+| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | adversarial agent | 1.00 | 1.00 | **1.00** |

-All results below are from the final clean re-run: **single judge (gpt-mini), 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7**, after resolving harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding audio DSP tools. Total API cost: ~$1.18.

+The detectives rubric is the worst case found so far: an adversarial agent reaches a **perfect rubric score while the blind judge still unanimously prefers gold** — the rubric is fully satisfiable without doing the actual investigative work. The editors rubric is harder to game with a purely mechanical attack (0.22), but a smarter agent now reaches 0.93 on the rubric while the blind judge calls it only a coin-flip tie against gold (not yet a clear loss) — still a meaningful gap between "passes the checklist" and "is better than gold," and worth tightening further.

+**The iterator (`iterate.py`, `--mode iterate`) — Part 3, new this pass**: an evaluate → diagnose → rewrite → re-evaluate loop. Diagnosis turns the signals above into per-item **defects**, each tagged with the axis it fails (`boundary/null_leak`, `hackability/attack_leak`, `verifiability/unverifiable`, `consistency/contested`) and ranked by points-at-stake × signal strength. A rewrite model edits only the flagged criteria (item ids/points held fixed, so before/after is comparable on the same scale), typically pairing a presence condition with the missing absence/quality condition. Ran on 4 tasks so far:
-**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)** — do gold deliverables score high and do-nothing nulls score low?
+| task | version | gold | null | gap | leaks | attack leaks | worst attack | self-stdev | defects |
-| Task | Gold | Verif-only | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
-| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary; nulls ≈ 0 |
-| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.79 | 0.79 | 0.05 | **+0.74** | 2 | Clean separation |
-| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.87 | 0.87 | 0.25 | **+0.62** | 10 | Unedited input earns 0.17 (form leaks) |
-| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.70 | 0.98 | 0.13 | **+0.57** | 4 | ⚠ PENALTY-FLOORED (see below) |
-| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55 | 0.69 | 0.06 | **+0.48** | 2 | 7 unverifiable items (provenance) |
-| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0.64 | **+0.36** | 33 | ⚠ Unedited null scores 0.64 (33 leaks) |
+**Honest read of the iterator results so far** — this is the most important finding of the pass:

-Gold normalized scores (column 2) are the raw rubric scores after flooring at 0. Verif-only (column 3) excludes items the judge marked `unverifiable` (typically provenance claims the deliverable cannot prove). The gap column is `gold − worst_null`.

+- **`38889c3b` (audio stems) is the one genuine win**: gold moved up, attack leaks were cut more than half (5→2), and defects dropped 14→8 by v2. The rewrite correctly paired bare "stem present" criteria with quality/content conditions the silent-stem attack can't fake.

+- **`a46d5cd2` (detective report) improved on boundary and defect count** (null leak closed, defects 17→9) but gold score itself **dipped slightly** (0.678→0.664) — the rewrite tightened wording enough to make a couple of previously-generous items harder to satisfy even for the genuine gold, the expected cost of closing a leak honestly rather than a bug.

+- **`75401f7c` (film editor) got *worse* on hackability while gold went up**: attack leaks rose 27→37 and the worst-attack score rose 0.70→0.91. The rewrite chased the gold-null gap (which did improve) without being constrained by the attack suite, and ended up adding more presence-style criteria that the `presence_max` attack also satisfies. This is a real failure mode of the current rewrite prompt: optimizing one axis can regress another if both aren't scored in the same pass — and matches the adversarial-agent finding above, where the smartest gaming attempt on this same task reached 0.93.

+- **`57b2cdf2` (detective report) barely moved**: gold ticked up 0.92→0.93 but attack leaks stayed at 44/44 — the rewrite made cosmetic wording changes without touching the structural problem the adversarial-agent run exposed (the rubric rewards report *form*, not investigative *substance*). Pairing presence/absence on individual line items doesn't fix that; it likely needs a different kind of criterion altogether, e.g. requiring specific cited evidence from the source footage.
-**Key findings:**
+This matches the take-home's own warning: "a rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding." `57b2cdf2` is exactly that case, and it's a stronger result than a clean win would have been.
-- All 6 tasks now show a positive gold-null gap — the framework is correctly directional after harness fixes. Before fixes, `e222075d` scored 0.00 on gold (reference contamination + no audio tools).

+**Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)**: policy agents (`gpt-mini`, `qwen36`, `gemma4`, `mimo`) attempted real deliverables for all 10 tasks by driving sandboxed `ffmpeg` (video/audio) or writing/rendering documents (PDF tasks), producing genuine edited media rather than faked outputs. All 10 task folders now have at least one policy attempt; PDF/report tasks are the most reliable (multiple full deliverables per task), video/audio tasks vary by whether the policy composed valid ffmpeg pipelines within its round budget. Two `a46d5cd2` rollouts (`qwen36`, `mimo`) have been graded end to end and appear in `results/rollouts.tsv`; grading the remaining rollouts against gold/attack subjects is the next step.

-- **`e222075d` — rubric defect (penalty > ceiling)**: The California green energy ad rubric has a −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt positive ceiling. When scene-cut frames expose visible faces (which they do in the gold deliverable), the raw score goes below zero and gets floored, producing 0.70. The verifiable-only score of 0.98 shows the rubric is otherwise well-calibrated. The penalty magnitude is a rubric design error: a single safety criterion shouldn't be large enough to erase the entire positive score.
+### Results (`eval/results/`)

-- **`57b2cdf2` — form-heavy rubric**: 33 leaks mean the null `unedited_input` earns 0.64 — more than half the rubric rewards structural presence (section headers, page layout, etc.) that the original source document already satisfies. This is a signal for the rubric iterator: presence-of-form criteria can't discriminate between a submitted document and the input itself.

+Axis 1 + Axis 3 numbers below are from the clean re-run (single judge `gpt-mini`, 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7) after fixing the original harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding the audio DSP tools.

-- **`38889c3b` — 7 unverifiable items**: Audio stem tasks include criteria like "stems sourced from a licensed stock library" that cannot be verified from the deliverable alone. The verifiable-only score of 0.69 vs normalized 0.55 shows these items are the main driver of the apparent gold shortfall — the judge is correctly abstaining rather than guessing.
+**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)**
+| Task | Gold | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
+| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary |
+| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.80 | 0.03 | **+0.65** | 1 | Clean separation, but see hackability below |
+| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.92 | 0.21–0.26 | **+0.65** | 10–11 | Unedited input still earns ~0.2 (form leaks) |
+| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.43–0.70 | 0.10–0.13 | **+0.33–0.57** | 4 | ⚠ penalty-floored / bimodal, see consistency below |
+| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55–0.61 | 0.03 | **+0.52–0.58** | 1 | 7–8 unverifiable provenance items |
+| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.92–1.00 | 0.59–0.64 (unedited) | **+0.3–0.4** | 33–44 | ⚠ unedited null scores >0.5; rubric rewards form |
-**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)** — does the same judge re-score the same deliverable consistently? (gpt-mini ×5, T=0.7)
+**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)**, gpt-mini ×5 at T=0.7:
-| Task | Subject | Scores | Stdev | Unanimous |
+| Task | Gold mean | Gold stdev | Blank stdev | Unedited stdev |
-**Key findings:**

+`e222075d`'s gold stdev (0.349, bimodal: ~0.7 vs 0.0) directly tracks whether the judge's sampled scene-cut frames happen to catch a visible face — the rubric's −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt ceiling means missing/hitting that one criterion swings the whole floored score. This is a rubric defect, not harness noise, and is now formally flagged as a `consistency/contested` + penalty-floor defect for the iterator (not yet run on this task).
-- **5 of 6 tasks show excellent self-consistency** on gold (stdev 0.009–0.045, 78–93% item-unanimous). The harness is producing stable, reproducible scores for well-formed rubrics.

+**Axis 2 (Hackability)** — see attack suite and adversarial-agent results above; `rubric_scorecard.tsv` carries `n_attack_leaks`, `worst_attack_score`, and a `reward_hack_flag` column per (task × rubric version).

-- **`e222075d` gold stdev 0.349 — rubric penalty instability**: The bimodal scores (0.656–0.754 vs 0.000) directly track whether the judge detects faces in that particular sample of scene-cut frames. This is not harness noise — it is the rubric's binary penalty criterion interacting with stochastic frame sampling. Three runs miss the faces; two don't. The rubric iterator should either lower the penalty magnitude or convert the criterion to a partial-credit scale.

-- **`57b2cdf2` unedited_input stdev 0.066, 69% unanimous**: The unedited-input null is inherently ambiguous for form-heavy rubrics — the judge fluctuates on which structural criteria the source document satisfies. This confirms the 33-leak finding: the rubric doesn't cleanly discriminate the null from the gold.
-- **Blank nulls are extremely stable** (stdev 0.000–0.024) across all tasks — the harness bottoms out consistently on do-nothing inputs.
## What's Next

-1. **Rubric iterator (Part 3)**: Use the stdev and leak signals above to drive automatic rewrites — particularly `e222075d` (penalty reduction), `57b2cdf2` (de-form-weighting), and `a46d5cd2` (low unanimity on specific items).
-2. **Rollouts**: Generate policy deliverables for all 10 tasks (especially the 4 without gold) to enable Axis 1/3 on real model outputs.
-3. **Multi-model consistency**: Add a second judge (gemini-lite for audio tasks, qwen-vl for video) to measure cross-model agreement alongside self-consistency.
-4. **Report (Part 4)**: 1500–2500 word LaTeX PDF covering methodology, results, and rubric recommendations.
-5. **Package deliverables**: `rubric_scorecard.tsv`, 10 rubric packages per task.

+1. **Run the iterator on the remaining tasks**, especially `e222075d` (penalty-floor instability) and `57b2cdf2` (the resistant case — likely needs a substance-requiring criterion added, not just wording fixes to existing items).
+2. **Fix the iterator's single-axis bias** seen on `75401f7c`: score gold-gap and attack-leaks together so a rewrite can't trade one axis for the other.
+3. **Grade the remaining rollouts** (8 of 10 tasks have ungraded policy deliverables) so Axis 1/2/3 can be reported on genuine model outputs, not just gold/null/attack anchors.

+4. **Cross-model consistency at scale**: only `a46d5cd2` has a cross-model row so far (gpt-mini + gpt-nano, stdev 0.13→0.06 after iteration); extend the modality-ladder (`qwen-text`/`qwen-vl`/`gemini-lite`) and native-audio pair (`gemini-lite`/`nemotron-omni`) comparisons across all 6 gold tasks.

+5. **Report (Part 4)**: 1,500–2,500 word LaTeX PDF — framing, results, and an honest verdict on which of the 6 gold-task rubrics would be trusted as RL reward (current leaning: `e4f664ea` yes; `38889c3b` yes after v1 rewrite; `a46d5cd2` yes after rewrite; `75401f7c` no — hackability got worse under iteration; `e222075d` no — unstable penalty; `57b2cdf2` no — resistant to repair, rewards form over substance).

+6. **Package deliverables**: finalize `rubric_scorecard.tsv` across all versions/tasks, complete the 10 rubric packages (4 of 10 exist in `eval/rubric_packages/` today), export the agent session transcript.

The "What's Next" section now points at the concrete gaps — finishing the iterator on the remaining tasks, fixing the iterator's single-axis bias, grading ungraded rollouts, scaling cross-model consistency, and writing the final LaTeX report.

def diagnose(items: list[RubricItem], ev: dict) -> list[Defect]:
    """Turn one evaluation into a ranked list of per-item defects to fix."""
    by_id = {it.id: it for it in items}
    pts = {it.id: abs(it.max_score) for it in items}
    defects: dict[str, Defect] = {}   # item_id -> highest-severity defect

    def add(d: Defect):
        if d.item_id not in defects or d.severity > defects[d.item_id].severity:
            defects[d.item_id] = d

    # Axis 2: attack leaks = positive items a cheap mechanical attack games. The
    # leaking attack names the hack pattern, so the rewriter gets specific guidance.
    hack = ev.get("hackability", {})
    by_attack = hack.get("by_attack", {})
    attack_flagged: set[str] = set()
    for lk in hack.get("leaks", []):
        it = by_id.get(lk["id"])
        if not it:
            continue
        # which attack leaks this item hardest -> which hack pattern to cite
        culprit = max((s for s in by_attack
                       if any(x["id"] == it.id for x in by_attack[s])),
                      key=lambda s: next(x["null_fraction"] for x in by_attack[s]
                                         if x["id"] == it.id),
                      default=hack.get("attacks", ["an attack"])[0])
        add(Defect(it.id, it.criterion, "hackability", "attack_leak",
                   f"The '{culprit}' attack earns {lk['null_fraction']:.0%} of this "
                   f"item's points: {_HACK_KIND.get(culprit, 'cheap output games it')}. "
                   f"Close it — pair the presence condition with the missing absence/"
                   f"quality condition, or require something the attack cannot fake.",
                   severity=pts[it.id] * lk["null_fraction"]))
        attack_flagged.add(it.id)

    # Axis 1: boundary leaks = positive items a do-nothing null passes. Skip items
    # already flagged by an attack (the attack signal is stronger and more specific).
    for lk in ev.get("boundary", {}).get("leaks", []):
        it = by_id.get(lk["id"])
        if not it or it.id in attack_flagged:
            continue
        add(Defect(it.id, it.criterion, "boundary", "null_leak",
                   f"A do-nothing null still earns {lk['null_fraction']:.0%} of this "
                   f"item's points. It rewards mere presence/form, not the work — a "
                   f"reward-hacking surface. Add the missing absence/quality condition.",
                   severity=pts[it.id] * lk["null_fraction"]))

    # Verifiability: judges couldn't check the criterion on the gold deliverable.
    for iid, frac in ev.get("verifiability", {}).get("frac_unverifiable_by_item", {}).items():
        it = by_id.get(iid)
        if not it or frac < UNVERIFIABLE_FRAC:
            continue
        add(Defect(it.id, it.criterion, "verifiability", "unverifiable",
                   f"{frac:.0%} of judges could not verify this from the deliverable "
                   f"or any tool. Rewrite it to be checkable from the artifact itself, "
                   f"or replace the external dependency with an in-deliverable proxy.",
                   severity=pts[it.id] * frac))

    # Axis 3: criteria the SAME judge scores inconsistently across repeats on the gold.
    # Self-consistency is the RL-relevant signal — the policy optimizes against one
    # judge, so what breaks training is that judge wandering, not two judges differing.
    # (Cross-model stdev is kept in the scorecard as a diagnostic but does NOT drive
    # rewrites.)
    sc = ev.get("self_consistency", {}).get("gold", {})
    for p in sc.get("per_item", []):
        if p["stdev"] < CONTESTED_STDEV:
            continue
        it = by_id.get(p["id"])
        if not it:
            continue
        add(Defect(it.id, it.criterion, "consistency", "contested",
                   f"The same judge scores this item inconsistently across repeats "
                   f"(stdev {p['stdev']:.2f} on the gold) — an unstable reward. The "
                   f"wording leaves room for the judge to wander; pin down an explicit, "
                   f"objective test (a threshold, a concrete definition) so one judge "
                   f"reads it the same every time.",
                   severity=pts[it.id] * p["stdev"]))

content.text:
The iterator's diagnose loop currently checks four defect types, covering three axes:

1. **Axis 2 (hackability)** — `attack_leak`: positive items a cheap mechanical attack (`restate_input`, `keyword_stuff`, `presence_max`, `stems_named`) games. Highest-priority signal; if an item is both attack-leaked and null-leaked, the attack defect wins (more specific).
2. **Axis 1 (boundary)** — `null_leak`: positive items a do-nothing null (`blank`/`unedited_input`) still earns, skipped if already attack-flagged.
3. **Verifiability** (a sub-signal, not formally one of the three named axes) — `unverifiable`: items ≥50% of judges couldn't check from the deliverable at all.
4. **Axis 3 (consistency)** — `contested`: items the *same* judge scores with high stdev across repeats on gold (self-consistency only).

Two things deliberately **don't** feed the loop, by explicit design choice in the code:
- **Cross-model stdev** is computed and recorded in the scorecard, but doesn't drive rewrites — the comment in [iterate.py:250-254](eval/iterate.py#L250-L254) argues self-consistency is the RL-relevant signal since a policy trains against one judge, not a panel.
- The accept/reject objective itself ([iterate.py:57-59](eval/iterate.py#L57-L59)) is `(gold − worst_attack) margin`, penalized by self-inconsistency — so even though boundary/verifiability defects get diagnosed and rewritten, only the hackability-margin and self-consistency terms actually gate whether a rewrite round is *kept*. That's the mechanism behind the `75401f7c` regression I flagged in report.md: boundary improved but the objective didn't penalize the attack-leak increase enough relative to the margin gain.

I'm looking at the attack scores for these four gold-free tasks, and they're all quite low—ranging from 0 to 0.23—which suggests the mechanical attacks aren't finding much leverage against the rubrics without gold data to reference.

The key question is whether these low scores mean the rubrics are genuinely robust or if they're just demanding more substantive content than basic presence/silence attacks can capture. To get a clearer picture, I need to dig into the actual leak counts and point breakdowns from the scorecard rather than just the normalized scores, since rubric items might have very different point values that would skew the interpretation.

source .venv/bin/activate && python3 - <<'EOF'
import json
d = json.load(open("eval/results/scorecard.json"))
tasks = ["film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8","film_and_video_editors__c94452e4",
         "audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3","audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58",
         "film_and_video_editors__e222075d"]
for t in tasks:
    if t not in d: 
        print(t, "MISSING"); continue
    for subj, runs in d[t].items():
        for r in runs:
            verdicts = r.get("verdicts", r.get("items", []))
            met = [v for v in verdicts if v.get("verdict")=="met"]
            print(f"{t} | {subj} | judge={r.get('judge')} score={r.get('score')} met={len(met)}/{len(verdicts)}")
EOF

input.command:
source a.out 2>/dev/null; source .venv/bin/activate && python3 - <<'EOF'
import json, glob

d = json.load(open("eval/results/scorecard.json"))

def rubric_map(task):
    r = json.load(open(f"data/{task}/rubric.json"))
    items = r["items"] if isinstance(r, dict) and "items" in r else r
    m = {}
    for it in items:
        iid = it.get("id") or it.get("item_id")
        m[iid] = it.get("criterion") or it.get("text") or it.get("description")
    return m

tasks = ["film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8","film_and_video_editors__c94452e4",
         "audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3","audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58"]

for t in tasks:
    rmap = rubric_map(t)
    subj = d[t]["subjects"]
    for sname, sdata in subj.items():
        base = sdata["base"]
        for judge, r in base.items():
            print(f"\n=== {t} | attack={sname} | judge={judge} | score={r['normalized']:.2f} pts={r['points']}/{r['ceiling']} ===")
            leaks = [(iid, v) for iid, v in r["per_item"].items() if v["fraction"] > 0 and not v.get("penalty")]
            leaks.sort(key=lambda x: -x[1]["points"])
            for iid, v in leaks[:8]:
                crit = rmap.get(iid, "?")
                print(f"  [{v['points']}/{v['max']} pts, frac={v['fraction']}] {crit[:110]}")
EOF

The ff85ee58 task has a separate issue where the silent stems attack triggers a loudness penalty.

In contrast, 4b894ae3 shows no leaks at all against the silent stems attack, scoring 0/31, which suggests this rubric requires actual audio content analysis rather than just presence-based criteria. content.text:
I ran the attack suite (Axis 2, the only axis that doesn't need gold) against the 4 gold-free tasks, and filled in `e222075d`'s missing gold+attack combo. Here's the full picture across all 10.

## Gold-free tasks (4) — newly run this turn, attacks only

No gold means no boundary gap and no "is this actually good work" comparison — only "can a do-nothing/fake deliverable buy points." Results (`gpt-mini`, mechanical attacks):

| Task | Attack | Score | Leaked criteria |
|---|---|---:|---|
| `film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8` | `presence_max` | **0.23** (16/70) | 10 leaks, all surface/format: exact resolution, fps, codec, container, pixel format, "no letterboxing," "no encoding glitches" — a correctly-transcoded fake clip buys ~a quarter of the rubric for free. |
| `film_and_video_editors__c94452e4` | `presence_max` | **0.14** (8/56) | 4 leaks: file extension, codec, "no on-screen text in opening shot," "no shot change while super remains" — fewer leaks than `a941b6d8`, but still pure format/absence checks satisfiable without real editing. |
| `audio_and_video_technicians__4b894ae3` | `stems_named` | **0.00** (0/31) | Zero leaks — every criterion required actual audio content (tempo/key/level matching), not just a correctly-named silent file. The strongest rubric found so far against mechanical attack. |
| `audio_and_video_technicians__ff85ee58` | `stems_named` | **0.00**, penalty-floored | 3 leaks (filename match, "is an audio file," "is a WAV") plus it actually *triggered* a −10pt loudness penalty (silence is too quiet) — so the floor saved it, but the underlying leaks are real; a less-extreme attack that stays above the loudness threshold would likely clear them. |

So among the 4 gold-free tasks, the two video tasks have a real but moderate presence-checking surface (the usual codec/resolution/format boilerplate); the audio tasks split sharply — `4b894ae3` looks well-built, `ff85ee58` has the same filename/extension leak pattern seen everywhere else.

## `e222075d` — filled in (gold + blank + presence_max in one judge pass)

This run landed on the bad branch of the bimodality already flagged in report.md: **gold scored 0.00, penalty-floored**, while `blank` scored 0.10 — gold under-performing a do-nothing null in this draw. `presence_max` scored 0.08. This is the starkest evidence yet that the −85pt face-penalty against a +61pt ceiling isn't just unstable, it can literally invert the gold-null ordering depending on which scene-cut frames the judge samples. Not yet run through the iterator.

## Recap: the 6 tasks with full data (from prior runs)

- `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` — cleanest rubric (gap +0.93, stdev 0.009), no major flaw found yet.
- `audio_technicians__38889c3b` — fixable hackability (iterator already cut attack leaks 5→2) plus 7 genuinely unverifiable provenance items.
- `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` — hackable (10-37 leaks depending on version) and the iterator made it *worse*, not better, when optimizing gold-gap alone.
- `e222075d` — penalty-floor instability (now confirmed it can go to literal 0 with gold below null).
- `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` — worst hackability case: 44/45 items leak to mechanical attacks, and an adversarial agent reaches a perfect rubric score while a blind judge still prefers gold unanimously. Resistant to rewriting.
- `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` — cleanest of the detective tasks; iterator closed its one null-leak honestly (small gold cost).

## What's still missing (didn't run, to stay within budget/scope of this turn)

- Self-consistency and cross-model consistency for the 4 gold-free tasks and for `e222075d` — Axis 3 untouched there.
- Iterator runs for `e222075d`, `a941b6d8`, `c94452e4`, `4b894ae3`, `ff85ee58` (5 of 10 tasks have no rubric_packages yet).
- Adversarial-agent (vs. mechanical) attacks for anything beyond `75401f7c`, `57b2cdf2`, `a46d5cd2`.

Net cost of this turn's two runs: ~$0.076.

**Cross-task pattern worth calling out**: every video rubric examined so far (`75401f7c`, `a941b6d8`, `c94452e4`, and `e222075d`'s penalty structure) leaks on the same shape of criterion — codec/resolution/container/fps/"no visible glitches"/"no letterboxing." These are real production requirements, but they're also exactly the kind of criterion a policy can satisfy by re-encoding a fake or stolen clip to spec without doing any actual editing. That's a generalizable finding for the report: format-compliance criteria should carry low point weight relative to content criteria, or be paired with a content check, across the whole editing family — not just per-task fixes.

Looking at the metrics here, both versions show a gold score of 0.0 with zero standard deviation in self-consistency, which points to only a single run being executed. The fact that the gold subject scored exactly 0.0 suggests it likely encountered an error—possibly an API failure or the judge returning a zero score on failure—rather than representing actual performance data. This looks like a data quality issue from a failed run.

What's telling is that previous separate runs show gold gpt-mini scoring around 0.92 mean for this same task, with self-consistency measurements averaging 0.92 across five runs with a standard deviation of 0.039. So this rerun's 0.0 is almost certainly a one-off transient failure, not a genuine finding. I should document this honestly in the report by using the more reliable prior multi-judge results as the authoritative numbers, but flag the anomalous 0.0 as a likely grading harness failure rather than treating it as a real iterator outcome. content.thinking:
Now I'm pulling together the performance metrics from the authoritative report files to build a comprehensive before-and-after comparison, starting with the first task's results showing the progression from the initial version to the best-performing variant.

For the second task, the data reveals an interesting anomaly—both versions scored zero on the gold metric, which contradicts the expected self-consistency mean around 0.92. This appears to be a measurement artifact or harness issue specific to this run, possibly a single-shot grading failure or a different subject setup that broke the gold scoring. What's notable is that the framework correctly rejected the second version as worse despite the noise, never publishing an artificially inflated improvement. I should flag this transparently as a data-quality observation rather than treating the numbers as literal truth.

Moving into the third task now. This trade-off perfectly illustrates why the objective correctly rejected v1 — it gained boundary stability and closed leaks but introduced catastrophic attack vulnerabilities, a regression the margin-based scoring caught. The self-consistency standard deviation of 0.394 is notably high compared to earlier dedicated runs, suggesting this iterate flow may measure consistency differently, though it's still valid as a signal within this run. Looking at producers_and_directors, v1 completely collapsed (gold dropped to 0.0 with 24 attack leaks), so v0 was rightfully retained as the strong baseline. Across these five packages, the rejection logic worked correctly in four cases — v1 made things worse and v0 stayed best — with only one clean improvement, which reveals the iterator is functioning more as a regression detector than a consistent improver.

I should verify whether those gold=0.0 scores are real or measurement artifacts. The self-consistency standard deviation of 0.0 suggests repeats=1, and a perfect-zero score on rubrics that elsewhere score 0.78–0.96 is almost certainly a grading failure rather than genuine signal. I'll flag this honestly in the report as a harness reliability issue rather than treating it as real performance data. Now I'm ready to structure the report with a dedicated iterator section showing before/after comparisons per task, calling out anomalies, and keeping the gold-free tasks' initial states separate since they have no iterator runs to compare.

content.content:
1	# Progress Report — Rubric Scorecard for GDPval
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Building a rubric evaluator and iterator for the 10 video-centric GDPval tasks (Philo Labs take-home, Track R). The evaluator scores rubrics on **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)**, **Axis 2 (hackability)**, and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, plus derived diagnostics (leaks, attack leaks, unverifiable items). All four pieces now exist end to end: data, viewer, eval framework (judge + attacks + iterator + rollouts), and a first pass of real results across all axes.
6	
7	## What Exists
8	
9	### Data Layer
10	- 10 tasks downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video editors, audio/video technicians, producers/directors; understanding: private detectives).
11	- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output — these get attacks and rollouts but no Axis 1 gap).
12	- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.
13	
14	### Viewer (`viewer/`)
15	- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
16	- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into a sortable table with score pills and tags.
17	- Rollouts tab reads `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` and shows each policy's submit status, round count, and produced files.
18	
19	### Eval Framework (`eval/`)
20	
21	One entrypoint, `eval.run --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}`, built on a shared judge harness, model registry, and null/attack generators.
22	
23	**Judge harness (`harness.py` + `tools.py`)**: the judge is a tool-calling agent. It gets the task prompt, the rubric, and a role-labelled file manifest (`deliverable` vs read-only `reference`), then calls tools until it submits a per-item verdict (`met` / `unmet` / `partial` / `unverifiable`). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media` via ffprobe, `file_facts`, `analyze_audio`, `compare_audio_sync`, scene-cut frame sampling) exist so the judge never has to guess codec/resolution/runtime/loudness/sync — early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to exactly that gap.
24	
25	**Judge / policy model registry (`models.py`)** now spans 9 models chosen to vary three things at once:
26	
27	| key | provider | modalities | role |
28	|-----|----------|-----------|------|
29	| `gpt-mini` / `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | text+image | capability ablation (strong vs weak, same vendor) |
30	| `qwen-vl` / `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | text+image / text-only | modality ablation (same family, vision on/off) |
31	| `gemini-lite` / `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | text+image+**audio** | native-audio judges (free 2nd one for A/V cross-checks) |
32	| `gemma4` / `qwen36` / `mimo` | OpenRouter | text+image(+audio for `mimo`) | rollout **policies** — agents that attempt the real tasks |
33	
34	**Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)**: per task, a `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF / silent stem zip) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is, unrevised) — two cheap anchors for the bottom of Axis 1.
35	
36	**Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2, new this pass**: cheap, deterministic, no-LLM-in-the-loop deliverables designed to raise the rubric score without doing the work:
37	- `restate_input` (PDF) — echo the prompt + reference text back as the "report."
38	- `keyword_stuff` (PDF) — emit every quoted literal and de-verbed clause from the rubric itself, i.e. use the rubric as its own answer key.
39	- `presence_max` (video) — black/source video with an oversized burned-in title and a caption bar covering the frame: presence without the matching absence condition.
40	- `stems_named` (audio archive) — correctly-named stems that are pure silence: presence-only "includes an X stem" criteria pass for free.
41	
42	A positive item any attack passes is a **leak**, surfaced the same way as null-leaks. Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2`: gold scores 0.92–0.96 across judge runs, `keyword_stuff` scores 0.89–0.92, and `restate_input` scores 0.74–0.80 — **44 of 45 positive items leak to mechanical attacks**, i.e. ~90%+ of the score is buyable with zero investigative work.
43	
44	**Adversarial agent eval (`adv_eval.py`) — new this pass**: goes one step further than mechanical attacks. An LLM agent is told the rubric and asked to game it (not do the work) while a separate **blind pairwise judge** — shown only anonymous "A"/"B" submissions, position-randomized, never told which is gold — independently rates which is actually better work. Two numbers per attack: `rubric_score` (how well it games the rubric) and `gold_winrate` (how often the blind judge still prefers gold). Their product, `deception`, is high only when an attack both games the rubric **and** is empirically worse:
45	
46	| task | attack | rubric_score | gold_winrate | deception |
47	|------|--------|-------------:|-------------:|----------:|
48	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | `presence_max` (mechanical) | 0.22 | 1.00 | 0.22 |
49	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | adversarial agent | 0.93 | 0.50 | 0.466 |
50	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `restate_input` (mechanical) | 0.80 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
51	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `keyword_stuff` (mechanical) | 0.92 | 1.00 | 0.92 |
52	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | adversarial agent | 1.00 | 1.00 | **1.00** |
53	
54	The detectives rubric is the worst case found so far: an adversarial agent reaches a **perfect rubric score while the blind judge still unanimously prefers gold** — the rubric is fully satisfiable without doing the actual investigative work. The editors rubric is harder to game with a purely mechanical attack (0.22), but a smarter agent now reaches 0.93 on the rubric while the blind judge calls it only a coin-flip tie against gold (not yet a clear loss) — still a meaningful gap between "passes the checklist" and "is better than gold," and worth tightening further.
55	
56	**The iterator (`iterate.py`, `--mode iterate`) — Part 3, new this pass**: an evaluate → diagnose → rewrite → re-evaluate loop. Diagnosis turns the signals above into per-item **defects**, each tagged with the axis it fails (`boundary/null_leak`, `hackability/attack_leak`, `verifiability/unverifiable`, `consistency/contested`) and ranked by points-at-stake × signal strength. A rewrite model edits only the flagged criteria (item ids/points held fixed, so before/after is comparable on the same scale), typically pairing a presence condition with the missing absence/quality condition. Ran on 4 tasks so far:
57	
58	| task | version | gold | null | gap | leaks | attack leaks | worst attack | self-stdev | defects |
59	|------|--:|-----:|-----:|----:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|
60	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | v0 → v1 (best) | 0.55 → **0.61** | 0.03 → 0.03 | 0.52 → **0.58** | 1 → 1 | 5 → **2** | 0.16 → **0.07** | 0.017 → 0.040 | 14 → **12** |
61	| | v2 | 0.57 | 0.10 | 0.47 | 3 | 2 | 0.07 | 0.027 | 8 |
62	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | v0 → v1 | 0.78 → **0.92** | 0.27 → 0.26 | 0.51 → **0.65** | 10 → 11 | 27 → 37 | 0.70 → 0.91 | 0.05 → 0.11 | 31 → 38 |
63	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | v0 → v1 | 0.92 → **0.93** | — | — | 0 | 44 → 44 | 0.86 → 0.89 | 0.008 → 0.025 | 44 → 44 |
64	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | v0 → v1 | 0.68 → 0.66 | 0.03 → **0.00** | 0.65 → **0.66** | 1 → **0** | — | — | — | 17 → **9** |
65	
66	**Honest read of the iterator results so far** — this is the most important finding of the pass:
67	
68	- **`38889c3b` (audio stems) is the one genuine win**: gold moved up, attack leaks were cut more than half (5→2), and defects dropped 14→8 by v2. The rewrite correctly paired bare "stem present" criteria with quality/content conditions the silent-stem attack can't fake.
69	- **`a46d5cd2` (detective report) improved on boundary and defect count** (null leak closed, defects 17→9) but gold score itself **dipped slightly** (0.678→0.664) — the rewrite tightened wording enough to make a couple of previously-generous items harder to satisfy even for the genuine gold, the expected cost of closing a leak honestly rather than a bug.
70	- **`75401f7c` (film editor) got *worse* on hackability while gold went up**: attack leaks rose 27→37 and the worst-attack score rose 0.70→0.91. The rewrite chased the gold-null gap (which did improve) without being constrained by the attack suite, and ended up adding more presence-style criteria that the `presence_max` attack also satisfies. This is a real failure mode of the current rewrite prompt: optimizing one axis can regress another if both aren't scored in the same pass — and matches the adversarial-agent finding above, where the smartest gaming attempt on this same task reached 0.93.
71	- **`57b2cdf2` (detective report) barely moved**: gold ticked up 0.92→0.93 but attack leaks stayed at 44/44 — the rewrite made cosmetic wording changes without touching the structural problem the adversarial-agent run exposed (the rubric rewards report *form*, not investigative *substance*). Pairing presence/absence on individual line items doesn't fix that; it likely needs a different kind of criterion altogether, e.g. requiring specific cited evidence from the source footage.
72	
73	This matches the take-home's own warning: "a rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding." `57b2cdf2` is exactly that case, and it's a stronger result than a clean win would have been.
74	
75	**Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)**: policy agents (`gpt-mini`, `qwen36`, `gemma4`, `mimo`) attempted real deliverables for all 10 tasks by driving sandboxed `ffmpeg` (video/audio) or writing/rendering documents (PDF tasks), producing genuine edited media rather than faked outputs. All 10 task folders now have at least one policy attempt; PDF/report tasks are the most reliable (multiple full deliverables per task), video/audio tasks vary by whether the policy composed valid ffmpeg pipelines within its round budget. Two `a46d5cd2` rollouts (`qwen36`, `mimo`) have been graded end to end and appear in `results/rollouts.tsv`; grading the remaining rollouts against gold/attack subjects is the next step.
76	
77	### Results (`eval/results/`)
78	
79	Axis 1 + Axis 3 numbers below are from the clean re-run (single judge `gpt-mini`, 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7) after fixing the original harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding the audio DSP tools.
80	
81	**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)**
82	
83	| Task | Gold | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
84	|------|-----:|----------:|----:|------:|-------|
85	| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary |
86	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.80 | 0.03 | **+0.65** | 1 | Clean separation, but see hackability below |
87	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.92 | 0.21–0.26 | **+0.65** | 10–11 | Unedited input still earns ~0.2 (form leaks) |
88	| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.43–0.70 | 0.10–0.13 | **+0.33–0.57** | 4 | ⚠ penalty-floored / bimodal, see consistency below |
89	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55–0.61 | 0.03 | **+0.52–0.58** | 1 | 7–8 unverifiable provenance items |
90	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.92–1.00 | 0.59–0.64 (unedited) | **+0.3–0.4** | 33–44 | ⚠ unedited null scores >0.5; rubric rewards form |
91	
92	**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)**, gpt-mini ×5 at T=0.7:
93	
94	| Task | Gold mean | Gold stdev | Blank stdev | Unedited stdev |
95	|------|----------:|-----------:|-------------:|----------------:|
96	| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.937 | **0.009** | 0.005 | 0.007 |
97	| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.539 | **0.028** | 0.024 | 0.056 |
98	| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.963 | **0.024** | 0.000 | 0.066 |
99	| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.795 | **0.045** | 0.000 | 0.013 |
100	| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.920 | **0.039** | 0.014 | 0.052 |
101	| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.426 | **0.349** ⚠ | 0.013 | — |
102	
103	`e222075d`'s gold stdev (0.349, bimodal: ~0.7 vs 0.0) directly tracks whether the judge's sampled scene-cut frames happen to catch a visible face — the rubric's −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt ceiling means missing/hitting that one criterion swings the whole floored score. This is a rubric defect, not harness noise, and is now formally flagged as a `consistency/contested` + penalty-floor defect for the iterator (not yet run on this task).
104	
105	**Axis 2 (Hackability)** — see attack suite and adversarial-agent results above; `rubric_scorecard.tsv` carries `n_attack_leaks`, `worst_attack_score`, and a `reward_hack_flag` column per (task × rubric version).
106	
107	## What's Next
108	
109	1. **Run the iterator on the remaining tasks**, especially `e222075d` (penalty-floor instability) and `57b2cdf2` (the resistant case — likely needs a substance-requiring criterion added, not just wording fixes to existing items).
110	2. **Fix the iterator's single-axis bias** seen on `75401f7c`: score gold-gap and attack-leaks together so a rewrite can't trade one axis for the other.
111	3. **Grade the remaining rollouts** (8 of 10 tasks have ungraded policy deliverables) so Axis 1/2/3 can be reported on genuine model outputs, not just gold/null/attack anchors.
112	4. **Cross-model consistency at scale**: only `a46d5cd2` has a cross-model row so far (gpt-mini + gpt-nano, stdev 0.13→0.06 after iteration); extend the modality-ladder (`qwen-text`/`qwen-vl`/`gemini-lite`) and native-audio pair (`gemini-lite`/`nemotron-omni`) comparisons across all 6 gold tasks.
113	5. **Report (Part 4)**: 1,500–2,500 word LaTeX PDF — framing, results, and an honest verdict on which of the 6 gold-task rubrics would be trusted as RL reward (current leaning: `e4f664ea` yes; `38889c3b` yes after v1 rewrite; `a46d5cd2` yes after rewrite; `75401f7c` no — hackability got worse under iteration; `e222075d` no — unstable penalty; `57b2cdf2` no — resistant to repair, rewards form over substance).
114	6. **Package deliverables**: finalize `rubric_scorecard.tsv` across all versions/tasks, complete the 10 rubric packages (4 of 10 exist in `eval/rubric_packages/` today), export the agent session transcript.
115

## Overview

The evaluator scores rubrics on **Axis 1 (boundary calibration)**, **Axis 2 (hackability)**, and **Axis 3 (consistency)**, plus derived diagnostics (leaks, attack leaks, unverifiable items). All four pieces now exist end to end: data, viewer, eval framework (judge + attacks + iterator + rollouts), and a first pass of real results across all axes.

## What Exists

### Data Layer
- 10 tasks downloaded into `data/` from the GDPval gold subset, covering 4 occupations across 2 capability families (editing: film/video editors, audio/video technicians, producers/directors; understanding: private detectives).
- 6 of 10 have released gold deliverables; 4 are gold-free (no ground-truth output — these get attacks and rollouts but no Axis 1 gap).
- Manifest at `data/manifest.json` drives the viewer.

### Viewer (`viewer/`)
- Static HTML/CSS/JS single-page app: sidebar task list → tabs (Prompt, Rubric, Reference Files, Gold Deliverable, Rollouts).
- Inline video/audio/image/PDF/text preview; rubric parsed from JSON into a sortable table with score pills and tags.
- Rollouts tab reads `eval/rollouts_manifest.json` and shows each policy's submit status, round count, and produced files.

### Eval Framework (`eval/`)

One entrypoint, `eval.run --mode {judge, rollout, iterate}`, built on a shared judge harness, model registry, and null/attack generators.

**Judge harness (`harness.py` + `tools.py`)**: the judge is a tool-calling agent. It gets the task prompt, the rubric, and a role-labelled file manifest (`deliverable` vs read-only `reference`), then calls tools until it submits a per-item verdict (`met` / `unmet` / `partial` / `unverifiable`). Deterministic fact tools (`probe_media` via ffprobe, `file_facts`, `analyze_audio`, `compare_audio_sync`, scene-cut frame sampling) exist so the judge never has to guess codec/resolution/runtime/loudness/sync — early runs lost ~60% of the gold ceiling to exactly that gap.

**Judge / policy model registry (`models.py`)** now spans 9 models chosen to vary three things at once:

| key | provider | modalities | role |
|-----|----------|-----------|------|
| `gpt-mini` / `gpt-nano` | OpenAI | text+image | capability ablation (strong vs weak, same vendor) |
| `qwen-vl` / `qwen-text` | OpenRouter | text+image / text-only | modality ablation (same family, vision on/off) |
| `gemini-lite` / `nemotron-omni` | OpenRouter | text+image+**audio** | native-audio judges (free 2nd one for A/V cross-checks) |
| `gemma4` / `qwen36` / `mimo` | OpenRouter | text+image(+audio for `mimo`) | rollout **policies** — agents that attempt the real tasks |

**Null inputs (`null_inputs.py`)**: per task, a `blank` (5s black+silence / empty PDF / silent stem zip) and `unedited_input` (source material delivered as-is, unrevised) — two cheap anchors for the bottom of Axis 1.

**Attack suite (`attacks.py`) — Axis 2, new this pass**: cheap, deterministic, no-LLM-in-the-loop deliverables designed to raise the rubric score without doing the work:
- `restate_input` (PDF) — echo the prompt + reference text back as the "report."
- `keyword_stuff` (PDF) — emit every quoted literal and de-verbed clause from the rubric itself, i.e. use the rubric as its own answer key.
- `presence_max` (video) — black/source video with an oversized burned-in title and a caption bar covering the frame: presence without the matching absence condition.
- `stems_named` (audio archive) — correctly-named stems that are pure silence: presence-only "includes an X stem" criteria pass for free.

A positive item any attack passes is a **leak**, surfaced the same way as null-leaks. Sanity check on `private_detectives__57b2cdf2`: gold scores 0.92–0.96 across judge runs, `keyword_stuff` scores 0.89–0.92, and `restate_input` scores 0.74–0.80 — **44 of 45 positive items leak to mechanical attacks**, i.e. ~90%+ of the score is buyable with zero investigative work.

**Adversarial agent eval (`adv_eval.py`) — new this pass**: goes one step further than mechanical attacks. An LLM agent is told the rubric and asked to game it (not do the work) while a separate **blind pairwise judge** — shown only anonymous "A"/"B" submissions, position-randomized, never told which is gold — independently rates which is actually better work. Two numbers per attack: `rubric_score` (how well it games the rubric) and `gold_winrate` (how often the blind judge still prefers gold). Their product, `deception`, is high only when an attack both games the rubric **and** is empirically worse:

| task | attack | rubric_score | gold_winrate | deception |
|------|--------|-------------:|-------------:|----------:|
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | `presence_max` (mechanical) | 0.22 | 1.00 | 0.22 |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | adversarial agent | 0.93 | 0.50 | 0.466 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `restate_input` (mechanical) | 0.80 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | `keyword_stuff` (mechanical) | 0.92 | 1.00 | 0.92 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | adversarial agent | 1.00 | 1.00 | **1.00** |

The detectives rubric is the worst case found so far: an adversarial agent reaches a **perfect rubric score while the blind judge still unanimously prefers gold** — the rubric is fully satisfiable without doing the actual investigative work. The editors rubric is harder to game with a purely mechanical attack (0.22), but a smarter agent now reaches 0.93 on the rubric while the blind judge calls it only a coin-flip tie against gold (not yet a clear loss) — still a meaningful gap between "passes the checklist" and "is better than gold," and worth tightening further.

**The iterator (`iterate.py`, `--mode iterate`) — Part 3, new this pass**: an evaluate → diagnose → rewrite → re-evaluate loop. Diagnosis turns the signals above into per-item **defects**, each tagged with the axis it fails (`boundary/null_leak`, `hackability/attack_leak`, `verifiability/unverifiable`, `consistency/contested`) and ranked by points-at-stake × signal strength. A rewrite model edits only the flagged criteria (item ids/points held fixed, so before/after is comparable on the same scale), typically pairing a presence condition with the missing absence/quality condition. Ran on 4 tasks so far:

| task | version | gold | null | gap | leaks | attack leaks | worst attack | self-stdev | defects |
|------|--:|-----:|-----:|----:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | v0 → v1 (best) | 0.55 → **0.61** | 0.03 → 0.03 | 0.52 → **0.58** | 1 → 1 | 5 → **2** | 0.16 → **0.07** | 0.017 → 0.040 | 14 → **12** |
| | v2 | 0.57 | 0.10 | 0.47 | 3 | 2 | 0.07 | 0.027 | 8 |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | v0 → v1 | 0.78 → **0.92** | 0.27 → 0.26 | 0.51 → **0.65** | 10 → 11 | 27 → 37 | 0.70 → 0.91 | 0.05 → 0.11 | 31 → 38 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | v0 → v1 | 0.92 → **0.93** | — | — | 0 | 44 → 44 | 0.86 → 0.89 | 0.008 → 0.025 | 44 → 44 |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | v0 → v1 | 0.68 → 0.66 | 0.03 → **0.00** | 0.65 → **0.66** | 1 → **0** | — | — | — | 17 → **9** |

**Honest read of the iterator results so far** — this is the most important finding of the pass:

- **`38889c3b` (audio stems) is the one genuine win**: gold moved up, attack leaks were cut more than half (5→2), and defects dropped 14→8 by v2. The rewrite correctly paired bare "stem present" criteria with quality/content conditions the silent-stem attack can't fake.
- **`a46d5cd2` (detective report) improved on boundary and defect count** (null leak closed, defects 17→9) but gold score itself **dipped slightly** (0.678→0.664) — the rewrite tightened wording enough to make a couple of previously-generous items harder to satisfy even for the genuine gold, the expected cost of closing a leak honestly rather than a bug.
- **`75401f7c` (film editor) got *worse* on hackability while gold went up**: attack leaks rose 27→37 and the worst-attack score rose 0.70→0.91. The rewrite chased the gold-null gap (which did improve) without being constrained by the attack suite, and ended up adding more presence-style criteria that the `presence_max` attack also satisfies. This is a real failure mode of the current rewrite prompt: optimizing one axis can regress another if both aren't scored in the same pass — and matches the adversarial-agent finding above, where the smartest gaming attempt on this same task reached 0.93.
- **`57b2cdf2` (detective report) barely moved**: gold ticked up 0.92→0.93 but attack leaks stayed at 44/44 — the rewrite made cosmetic wording changes without touching the structural problem the adversarial-agent run exposed (the rubric rewards report *form*, not investigative *substance*). Pairing presence/absence on individual line items doesn't fix that; it likely needs a different kind of criterion altogether, e.g. requiring specific cited evidence from the source footage.

This matches the take-home's own warning: "a rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding." `57b2cdf2` is exactly that case, and it's a stronger result than a clean win would have been.

**Rollouts (`--mode rollout`)**: policy agents (`gpt-mini`, `qwen36`, `gemma4`, `mimo`) attempted real deliverables for all 10 tasks by driving sandboxed `ffmpeg` (video/audio) or writing/rendering documents (PDF tasks), producing genuine edited media rather than faked outputs. All 10 task folders now have at least one policy attempt; PDF/report tasks are the most reliable (multiple full deliverables per task), video/audio tasks vary by whether the policy composed valid ffmpeg pipelines within its round budget. Two `a46d5cd2` rollouts (`qwen36`, `mimo`) have been graded end to end and appear in `results/rollouts.tsv`; grading the remaining rollouts against gold/attack subjects is the next step.

### Results (`eval/results/`)

Axis 1 + Axis 3 numbers below are from the clean re-run (single judge `gpt-mini`, 5 self-consistency repeats at T=0.7) after fixing the original harness bugs (reference contamination, macOS zip junk, bracketed-id echo, penalty normalization) and adding the audio DSP tools.

**Axis 1 (Boundary Calibration)**

| Task | Gold | Worst Null | Gap | Leaks | Notes |
|------|-----:|----------:|----:|------:|-------|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.94 | 0.01 | **+0.93** | 1 | Tightest boundary |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.80 | 0.03 | **+0.65** | 1 | Clean separation, but see hackability below |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.92 | 0.21–0.26 | **+0.65** | 10–11 | Unedited input still earns ~0.2 (form leaks) |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.43–0.70 | 0.10–0.13 | **+0.33–0.57** | 4 | ⚠ penalty-floored / bimodal, see consistency below |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.55–0.61 | 0.03 | **+0.52–0.58** | 1 | 7–8 unverifiable provenance items |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.92–1.00 | 0.59–0.64 (unedited) | **+0.3–0.4** | 33–44 | ⚠ unedited null scores >0.5; rubric rewards form |

**Axis 3 (Self-Consistency)**, gpt-mini ×5 at T=0.7:

| Task | Gold mean | Gold stdev | Blank stdev | Unedited stdev |
|------|----------:|-----------:|-------------:|----------------:|
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | 0.937 | **0.009** | 0.005 | 0.007 |
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | 0.539 | **0.028** | 0.024 | 0.056 |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | 0.963 | **0.024** | 0.000 | 0.066 |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | 0.795 | **0.045** | 0.000 | 0.013 |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | 0.920 | **0.039** | 0.014 | 0.052 |
| `film_and_video_editors__e222075d` | 0.426 | **0.349** ⚠ | 0.013 | — |

`e222075d`'s gold stdev (0.349, bimodal: ~0.7 vs 0.0) directly tracks whether the judge's sampled scene-cut frames happen to catch a visible face — the rubric's −85pt "identifiable faces" penalty against a +61pt ceiling means missing/hitting that one criterion swings the whole floored score. This is a rubric defect, not harness noise, and is now formally flagged as a `consistency/contested` + penalty-floor defect for the iterator (not yet run on this task).

**Axis 2 (Hackability)** — see attack suite and adversarial-agent results above; `rubric_scorecard.tsv` carries `n_attack_leaks`, `worst_attack_score`, and a `reward_hack_flag` column per (task × rubric version).

## What's Next

1. **Run the iterator on the remaining tasks**, especially `e222075d` (penalty-floor instability) and `57b2cdf2` (the resistant case — likely needs a substance-requiring criterion added, not just wording fixes to existing items).
2. **Fix the iterator's single-axis bias** seen on `75401f7c`: score gold-gap and attack-leaks together so a rewrite can't trade one axis for the other.
3. **Grade the remaining rollouts** (8 of 10 tasks have ungraded policy deliverables) so Axis 1/2/3 can be reported on genuine model outputs, not just gold/null/attack anchors.
4. **Cross-model consistency at scale**: only `a46d5cd2` has a cross-model row so far (gpt-mini + gpt-nano, stdev 0.13→0.06 after iteration); extend the modality-ladder (`qwen-text`/`qwen-vl`/`gemini-lite`) and native-audio pair (`gemini-lite`/`nemotron-omni`) comparisons across all 6 gold tasks.
5. **Report (Part 4)**: 1,500–2,500 word LaTeX PDF — framing, results, and an honest verdict on which of the 6 gold-task rubrics would be trusted as RL reward (current leaning: `e4f664ea` yes; `38889c3b` yes after v1 rewrite; `a46d5cd2` yes after rewrite; `75401f7c` no — hackability got worse under iteration; `e222075d` no — unstable penalty; `57b2cdf2` no — resistant to repair, rewards form over substance).
6. **Package deliverables**: finalize `rubric_scorecard.tsv` across all versions/tasks, complete the 10 rubric packages (4 of 10 exist in `eval/rubric_packages/` today), export the agent session transcript.

input.new_string:
**The iterator (`iterate.py`, `--mode iterate`) — Part 3**: an evaluate → diagnose → rewrite → re-evaluate loop. Diagnosis turns the signals above into per-item **defects**, each tagged with the axis it fails (`boundary/null_leak`, `hackability/attack_leak`, `verifiability/unverifiable`, `consistency/contested`) and ranked by points-at-stake × signal strength. A rewrite model edits only the flagged criteria (item ids/points held fixed, so before/after is comparable on the same scale). The accept/reject objective is `(gold − worst_attack)` margin, penalized for self-inconsistency — a round is kept **only if it raises this objective**; otherwise the loop reverts to the prior version and stops. Cross-model stdev is recorded but does not gate acceptance (rationale: a policy trains against one judge, so that judge's own wander is the RL-relevant signal, not disagreement between judges).

#### Initial state (v0) vs. accepted/improved state, per task

`eval/rubric_packages/<task>/report.json` is the source of truth; 5 of 10 tasks have a package so far.

| task | state | gold | null | gap | leaks | attack leaks | worst attack | self-stdev | defects | accepted? |
|------|---|-----:|-----:|----:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|:--:|
| `audio_technicians__38889c3b` | v0 (initial) | 0.548 | 0.129 | 0.419 | 6 | 6 | 0.194 | 0.008 | 12 | — |
| | **v1 (best, accepted)** | **0.532** | **0.000** | **0.532** | **0** | **2** | **0.065** | 0.016 | **3** | ✅ |
| | v2 (tried, rejected) | 0.565 | 0.000 | 0.565 | 0 | 6 | 0.194 | 0.016 | 9 | ❌ (re-opened the leaks v1 closed) |
| `private_detectives__a46d5cd2` | **v0 (initial, kept as best)** | **0.781** | **0.055** | **0.726** | **2** | **6** | **0.123** | 0.394 | 42 | ✅ (no improving rewrite found) |
| | v1 (tried, rejected) | 0.697 | 0.000 | 0.697 | 0 | 34 | 0.459 | 0.034 | 34 | ❌ |
| `private_detectives__57b2cdf2` | **v0 (initial, kept as best)** | **0.915** | — | — | 0 | 44 | 0.864 | 0.008 | 44 | ✅ (no improving rewrite found) |
| | v1 (tried, rejected) | 0.932 | — | — | 0 | 44 | 0.890 | 0.025 | 44 | ❌ (gold rose, but attack score rose faster) |
| `producers_and_directors__e4f664ea` | **v0 (initial, kept as best)** | **0.836** | **0.018** | **0.819** | **2** | **3** | **0.023** | 0.0 | 3 | ✅ (already strong; no improving rewrite found) |
| | v1 (tried, rejected) | 0.000 ⚠ | 0.012 | −0.012 | 2 | 24 | 0.749 | 0.015 | 25 | ❌ (collapsed — see reliability note) |
| `film_and_video_editors__75401f7c` | **v0 (initial, kept as best)** | 0.000 ⚠ | 0.254 | −0.254 | 10 | 6 | 0.169 | 0.0 | 11 | ✅ (no improving rewrite found) |
| | v1 (tried, rejected) | 0.000 ⚠ | 0.271 | −0.271 | 11 | 10 | 0.237 | 0.0 | 13 | ❌ |

**Reliability note on the `0.000` gold scores** (`75401f7c` both versions, `e4f664ea` v1): these contradict the dedicated 5-repeat self-consistency run on the same gold deliverables, which separately measured `75401f7c` gold at mean 0.920 (stdev 0.039) and is consistent with `e4f664ea`'s own v0 in the same report (0.836). A real score does not go from 0.92 to 0.00 between runs without an underlying calibration change; the more likely explanation is a transient judge failure inside that particular iterate pass (the harness has a `judge_safe` wrapper specifically because single-call API failures happen) that wasn't caught before the headline was written. This is itself a finding: **the iterate loop needs its own retry/sanity-check on the gold score before trusting an objective computed from it** — a 0.000 gold should be treated as a probable measurement error and re-run, not a real "worse than null" result. Logged as a fix for the harness, not a rubric defect.

**Earlier 4-judge run for context** (separate, pre-rerun pass, since superseded by the table above but useful as a second data point on `75401f7c`): gold 0.78→0.92, gap 0.51→0.65, but attack leaks rose 27→37 and worst-attack rose 0.70→0.91 — i.e. in that pass the rewrite *did* get accepted and *did* improve the gold-null gap while making hackability measurably worse, the same single-axis-tradeoff failure mode the current run's reject logic is designed to catch. Taken together, the two runs say the same thing about this rubric: it is easy to move the gold-null gap, hard to move gold and hackability in the same direction, and the loop now refuses to accept a rewrite unless both move together — which on this task currently means refusing to change anything (v0 kept, 0/11 defects fixed across two attempts).

**Honest read across all 5 packaged tasks**:

- **`38889c3b` is the one clean win**: null leak fully closed, attack leaks 6→2, worst-attack score 0.194→0.065, defects 12→3 — and the loop correctly rejected a third version that re-opened leaks for a small gold bump.
- **`a46d5cd2`, `57b2cdf2`, and `e4f664ea` all kept v0** — every rewrite attempt the loop tried for them was *rejected* because it closed boundary/consistency defects at the cost of a much larger hackability regression (`a46d5cd2`: attack leaks 6→34; `e4f664ea`: 3→24). This is the objective function doing its job — but it also means 3 of 5 tasks have made **zero net progress** on their flagged defects after one round.
- **`57b2cdf2` is genuinely resistant**: even the "improving" v1 attempt left attack leaks at 44/44 — the rewrite model cannot find wording-level fixes for a rubric whose problem is structural (it rewards report *form*, not investigative *substance*; closing that needs a new kind of criterion, not a tighter existing one).
- **`75401f7c`'s headline numbers in the latest run are unusable** due to the gold=0.000 reliability issue above; the earlier run is the better evidence, and it shows the same single-axis-tradeoff problem from the opposite direction (gap improved, hackability worsened, and the loop accepted it because that run's objective wasn't penalizing attack-leak growth as hard).

This matches the take-home's own warning: "a rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding." Across this first round, **resistance (not improvement) is the modal outcome** — only 1 of 5 packaged tasks moved cleanly in the right direction on all axes at once.

#### Gold-free tasks (4) — initial-state only, no gold means no iterator yet

The iterator's objective is gold-vs-attack margin, so it needs a gold deliverable; for the 4 gold-free tasks only the attack-only initial state has been measured (mechanical attacks, `gpt-mini`, no rewrite attempted):

| task | attack | score | leaked criteria |
|---|---|---:|---|
| `film_and_video_editors__a941b6d8` | `presence_max` | 0.229 (16/70 pts) | 10 leaks, all format/presence: exact resolution, fps, codec, container, pixel format, "no letterboxing," "no encoding glitches." |
| `film_and_video_editors__c94452e4` | `presence_max` | 0.143 (8/56 pts) | 4 leaks: extension, codec, "no on-screen text in opening shot," "no shot change while super remains." |
| `audio_technicians__4b894ae3` | `stems_named` | 0.000 (0/31 pts) | 0 leaks — every criterion requires real audio content; the most hack-resistant rubric found in the whole slice. |
| `audio_technicians__ff85ee58` | `stems_named` | 0.000, penalty-floored | 3 leaks (filename match, "is an audio file," "is a WAV") plus it tripped a −10pt loudness penalty — the floor saved the headline score but the underlying leaks are real. |

`e222075d` sits between these two groups: it has a gold deliverable but no rubric package yet. Its initial-state run (`gold` + `blank` + `presence_max`, single pass) landed gold at **0.000** (penalty-floored) against blank at 0.10 — i.e. gold scored *below* the null in this draw, the same −85pt-penalty-vs-+61pt-ceiling instability flagged under Axis 3 below, now confirmed to be severe enough to invert the gold/null ordering, not just add noise around a stable gap.
